items.title: Rome IV Criteria
items.contentHtml:
Rome IV criteria for IBS

  • Recurrent abdominal pain at least 1 day/week in the last 3 months, associated with ≥2 of: relationship with defecation, change in frequency of bowel movements, change in shape (appearance) of stool.
  • The criteria must be met during the last 3 months with onset of symptoms at least 6 months before of the diagnosis.
  • They do not require invasive tests if there are no warning signs; It is a symptom-based diagnosis.
  • Compared to Rome III, the “discomfort” requirement was eliminated and the pain frequency threshold was reduced from ≥3 days/month to ≥1 day/week.

items.title: Pain and defecation
items.contentHtml:
Abdominal pain related to defecation

  • Pain typically improves or worsens with defecation; The relationship is evaluated by asking if the pain is associated with a change in the frequency or consistency of bowel movements.
  • The pain can be crampy, located in the hypogastrium or diffuse; It is usually relieved after evacuation, but it is not mandatory that it disappear completely.
  • The relationship with defecation is a key Rome IV criterion to distinguish IBS from other causes of functional abdominal pain.
  • Pain that is not modified by defecation suggests a differential diagnosis such as inflammatory bowel disease or functional abdominal pain syndrome.

items.title: Change in frequency
items.contentHtml:
Change in stool frequency

  • A change is considered when the patient reports <3 stools/week (constipation) or >3 stools/day (diarrhea).
  • The change must be persistent and not explained by another cause (e.g., infection, medications).
  • Frequency is evaluated by the Bristol Scale; Types 1-2 suggest constipation, types 6-7 diarrhea.
  • In IBS, the frequency can alternate between both extremes; the predominant pattern defines the subtype.

items.title: Relief with defecation
items.contentHtml:
Relief with defecation

  • Relief of pain after evacuation is a Rome IV criterion; It is considered present if the pain decreases or disappears after defecation.
  • Complete relief is not required; partial improvement is sufficient to meet the criterion.
  • The absence of relief with defecation should raise suspicion of other entities such as dyschezia or proctalgia fugax.
  • On the examination, they ask: “Is the pain relieved by defecation?”; An affirmative answer guides SII.

items.title: IBS subtypes
items.contentHtml:
Subtypes according to intestinal habit

  • They are classified using the Bristol Scale on days with abnormal bowel movements: IBS-E (Bristol 1-2 in >25% of bowel movements and Bristol 6-7 in <25%), IBS-D (Bristol 6-7 in >25% and Bristol 1-2 in <25%), IBS-M (mixed: both >25%), IBS-NC (unclassifiable).
  • Subtype is determined with a 2-week symptom diary.
  • Treatment and prognosis vary by subtype; For example, rifaximin is indicated in IBS-D and IBS-M, not in IBS-C.

items.title: IBS with constipation
items.contentHtml:
Constipation predominance (IBS-C)

  • Bristol type 1-2 stools in >25% of stools and Bristol 6-7 in <25%.
  • First line: soluble fiber (psyllium) and antispasmodics (e.g., hyoscine).
  • If no response, consider lubiprostone (8-24 µg/day) or linaclotide (290 µg/day).
  • Avoid insoluble fiber (bran) which can worsen bloating.

items.title: IBS with diarrhea
items.contentHtml:
Predominance of diarrhea (IBS-D)

  • Stools of Bristol type 6-7 in >25% of stools and Bristol 1-2 in <25%.
  • First line: loperamide (2-8 mg/day) to control diarrhea, although it does not improve pain.
  • Second line: rifaximin 550 mg three times a day for 14 days (indicated in IBS without constipation).
  • Consider eluxadoline (75-100 mg twice a day) or ondansetron (4-8 mg/day) if there is urgency.

items.title: Warning signs
items.contentHtml:
Alarm signs

  • Alarm signs that require ruling out organic pathology: involuntary weight loss, rectal blood (occult or overt), fever, anemia, family history of colorectal cancer or inflammatory bowel disease, onset after age 50.
  • The presence of any of these signs requires colonoscopy and studies
  • In the absence of warning signs, routine colonoscopy is not needed to diagnose IBS.
  • weight loss >5% in 6 months is a classic alarm criterion.

items.title: Minimal testing
items.contentHtml:
Minimal diagnostic tests

  • Complete blood count (rule out anemia), TSH (rule out hyperthyroidism in IBS-D or hypothyroidism in IBS-C), and anti-transglutaminase antibodies (rule out celiac disease) in IBS-D.
  • Calprotectin is recommended fecal or PCR if inflammatory bowel disease is suspected.
  • No routine imaging or endoscopy is required if there are no warning signs.
  • In IBS-D, consider lactose test or breath test for bacterial overgrowth if suspected.

items.title: First line: fiber and antispasmodics
items.contentHtml:
First-line treatment

  • Soluble fiber (psyllium 10-20 g/day) improves stool consistency; insoluble fiber may worsen symptoms.
  • Anti-spasmodics such as hyoscine (10-20 mg three times a day) or mebeverine (135 mg three times a day) reduce abdominal pain.
  • Anti-spasmodics are most effective if taken 30-60 minutes before meals.
  • In IBS-C, add osmotic laxatives (polyethylene glycol) if not responding to fiber alone.

items.title: Rifaximin in IBS-D
items.contentHtml:
Rifaximin in IBS without constipation

  • Rifaximin is a non-absorbable antibiotic (550 mg three times a day for 14 days) indicated in IBS-D and IBS-M (without predominant constipation).
  • Its mechanism is to modulate the intestinal microbiota; It reduces gas production and improves diarrhea and bloating.
  • It can be repeated up to 3 cycles if there is recurrence of symptoms.
  • It is not indicated in IBS-C; In that subtype, linaclotide or lubiprostone is preferred.

items.foco: Relationship with stress and brain-gut axis
items.title: Stress and the brain-gut axis
items.contentHtml:
Relationship with stress and the brain-gut axis

  • The brain-gut axis connects the central and enteric nervous systems; Chronic stress alters motility, intestinal permeability and visceral perception.
  • Serotonin (5-HT) plays a key role: in IBS-D there is visceral hypersensitivity and increased release of 5-HT; in IBS-C there is decreased motility.
  • Treatments targeting the axis include cognitive-behavioral therapy, hypnotherapy and tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline 10-50 mg/day) to modulate pain perception.
  • serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are less effective. for pain; tricyclics are preferred in low doses.
